text: Stoic self-observation is not introspection in the modern therapeutic sense. Where contemporary reflection asks "how do I feel?", the Stoic asks "what did I assent to?" Aurelius inherited from Epictetus the concept of the hegemonikon — the ruling faculty of the mind — whose health depends on monitoring the quality of its own judgments, not its emotions. Emotions, for the Stoics, were consequences of prior assents: you did not simply feel anger; you first judged that an injury was real and significant. Self-observation, then, meant watching the moment of judgment before it calcified into reaction.
question: According to the passage, what does Stoic self-observation focus on, as distinct from modern introspection?

options.text: The emotional residue left after conflict
The quality of judgments made by the ruling faculty, not emotions themselves
The gap between social behavior and private feeling
The health of the body as a mirror of the mind's condition
